title-group.article-title: Prevalence and risk factors of orofacial trauma among child and adolescent athletes in Damascus, Syria: a cross-sectional study

contrib-group.aff: https://ror.org/03m098d13grid.8192.20000 0001 2353 3326Pediatric Dentistry Department, Faculty of Dentistry, Damascus University, Damascus, Syria

license.license-p: Open Access This article is licensed under a Creative Commons Attribution-NonCommercial-NoDerivatives 4.0 International License, which permits any non-commercial use, sharing, distribution and reproduction in any medium or format, as long as you give appropriate credit to the original author(s) and the source, provide a link to the Creative Commons licence, and indicate if you modified the licensed material. You do not have permission under this licence to share adapted material derived from this article or parts of it. The images or other third party material in this article are included in the article’s Creative Commons licence, unless indicated otherwise in a credit line to the material. If material is not included in the article’s Creative Commons licence and your intended use is not permitted by statutory regulation or exceeds the permitted use, you will need to obtain permission directly from the copyright holder. To view a copy of this licence, visit http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/.

sec.title: Background and objective
sec.p: Participation in sports among children and adolescents is increasing globally, accompanied by a rising incidence of orofacial dental trauma. However, limited data exist on this public health issue in Syria. This study aimed to determine the prevalence and associated risk factors of dental and facial trauma among young athletes in Damascus, Syria.

sec.title: Methods
sec.p: A cross-sectional study was conducted among 445 athletes aged 6–16 years, selected through multistage cluster sampling from eleven sports clubs in Damascus. Data collection was conducted through a structured questionnaire and clinical examinations based on World Health Organization guidelines. Chi-square tests and multivariate logistic regression analyses were used to assess associations between ODT and demographic, sport-specific, and behavioral variables.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The prevalence of orofacial dental trauma was 47.4%. Contact sports such as football and basketball showed a higher rate of trauma, while gymnastics and karate had lower rates. Higher risk of orofacial dental trauma was significantly associated with increased training session frequency (OR = 9.121, 95% CI: 3.088–26.942), longer experience years of more than five years (OR = 28.159, 95% CI: 10.270–77.211) (P < 0.001). The use of protective equipment was low, with only 33.9% of athletes reporting any use. However, wearing mouthguards was significantly associated with reduced risk of orofacial dental trauma (OR = 0.286, 95% CI: 0.141–0.577, P < 0.004).

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: The high prevalence of orofacial dental trauma (47.4%) among young athletes in Damascus, influenced by sport type, years of experience, training session frequency, and inconsistent use of protective gear, underscores the urgent need for preventive strategies. These include enforcing mandatory protective equipment policies, implementing educational programs, and conducting regular oral health screenings in sports settings. The findings provide a foundation for future research and policy development aimed at reducing oral injuries in youth sports.

sec.title: Supplementary Information
sec.p: The online version contains supplementary material available at 10.1186/s12889-025-26149-8.

custom-meta.meta-value: © BioMed Central Ltd., part of Springer Nature 2026

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: Sports participation among children and adolescents has increased substantially worldwide, offering significant health, developmental, and social benefits [1, 2]. However, this growing trend has been accompanied by a rise in sports-related injuries, particularly those affecting the face and oral region [3]. The face, being the most exposed and least protected part of the body during sports activities, accounts for approximately 11–40% of all sports-related injuries [4].

This high incidence renders orofacial injuries a considerable public health issue due to their potential long-term consequences, including functional impairments, aesthetic damage, and psychological effects [1, 3].

Athletes are reported to be 10% more susceptible to orofacial dental trauma (ODT) than non-athletes, with 56% of injuries occurring during training or competition [5]. A recent meta-analysis reported a 27.57% prevalence of dentofacial injuries in contact sports, with dental trauma being the most common type at 19.61% [6].

The most common sports-related facial injuries include soft tissue injury and fractures of critical facial structures such as the nasal bones, zygomatic arches, and mandible, as well as dental injuries [1, 2, 4, 6]. These injuries most often result from collisions, falls, and impacts with other players or objects or accidental blows to the face [6–8].

Approximately 40% of dental injuries occur during sports, with estimated annual treatment costs exceeding $2 million per million inhabitants. Identifying factors associated with sports-related dental trauma in children is essential for effective prevention [9].

Patterns of orofacial injuries vary depending on the sport. Contact sports such as basketball, football, and martial arts exhibit higher injury rates due to frequent physical contact and collisions [1, 4]. Adolescent athletes, particularly those undergoing intensive training, face a greater risk than younger children, probably because of higher competition levels and longer training durations [3, 5]. Training-related factors such as session duration, weekly frequency, and years of participation also influence injury rates, though these factors are less studied compared to demographic and sport-specific factors [1, 5].

Protective equipment, especially mouthguards, plays a key role in preventing dentofacial injuries [10]. Mouthguards help protect teeth from displacement and fractures, prevent soft oral tissues from lacerations, and may reduce concussion risks by absorbing impacts [2]. Despite their proven effectiveness, mouthguard use remains inconsistent, often due to discomfort and lack of awareness about their benefits [2, 3, 10].

While numerous epidemiological studies have examined sports-related orofacial trauma in countries such as Iran [11], Portugal [12], and the United States [13], evidence from low-resource and conflict-affected settings such as Syria remains scarce, leaving important gaps in understanding the local burden and risk factors of ODT.

Previous research focused on specific populations; for example, AlShayeb et al. [14] reported a 17.6% dental trauma prevalence among schoolchildren, and Al-Shiekh et al. [15] found a 9.1% prevalence linked to orotracheal intubation. However, no research has yet examined orofacial injuries among young athletes in Damascus.

This study aimed to address the gap by evaluating the prevalence of dental and facial injuries and identifying associated risk factors, including gender, age, sport-specific risks, training variables, and the use of protective equipment among young athletes in Damascus, Syria. Understanding these factors will help shape targeted prevention strategies and improve oral health outcomes for young athletes.

sec.title: Study design and settings
sec.p: This observational cross-sectional study aimed to assess the prevalence of sports-related dentofacial injuries and their associated risk factors among young athletes from sports clubs across Damascus. The study included children aged 6 to 16 actively engaged in organized sports, conducted between January 2024 and January 2025. All procedures adhered to relevant regulations and received approval from the Ethical Research Committee at Damascus University (IRB No. UDDS-2950–10452023/SRC-1550). Written permission was also obtained from the General Sports Federation of Syria. Informed consent was obtained from each participant’s guardian before data collection. The study followed the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) guidelines. (Supplementary File 1)

sec.title: Population sampling
sec.p: A multistage cluster sampling approach was employed to mitigate selection bias. The sampling frame comprised all officially registered sports clubs in Damascus, as listed by the General Sports Federation. Eleven clubs were randomly selected using a computer-generated simple random sampling method. Within each club, a systematic random sampling of eligible children was conducted from team lists to achieve proportionate representation by sport type and age group.

sec.title: Inclusion and exclusion criteria
sec.p: Participants were selected based on their parents’ or guardians’ signed informed consent. Eligible subjects were Syrian boys and girls aged 6 to 16, officially registered in one of the selected clubs and involved in regular training or competition for at least six months. Following eligibility screening, 18 children were excluded from the study: three with special needs, seven undergoing orthodontic treatment, two with craniofacial deformities, and six who declined the oral examination. The final sample, therefore, comprised 445 children who met all inclusion criteria.

sec.title: Study protocol and data collection
sec.p: A combination of structured interviews and clinical oral examinations was used for data collection to capture both current and past occurrences of trauma. Orofacial trauma was defined as any dental or soft tissue injury sustained resulting from sports participation and was recorded both when clinically visible during examination and when reported by participants/guardians in the structured questionnaire. The recall period extended across the athlete’s entire duration of participation in organized sports. For athletes with multiple injuries, only the presence or absence of trauma was considered in prevalence estimates, while detailed injury characteristics were documented separately.

A single calibrated examiner conducted clinical oral examinations to assess the dental and orofacial status of each participant, to reduce inter-examiner variability. The examination followed the World Health Organization (WHO) oral health survey guidelines [17] and aimed to identify both current and past dentofacial injuries. Dental injuries were categorized according to Andreasen’s criteria [18]. During the clinical assessment, the examiner evaluated the condition of the teeth and surrounding structures, documenting any visible signs of trauma such as fractures, dislocations, or soft tissue damage. In addition to identifying injuries present at the time of examination, previously treated or healed traumas were also recorded if reported by the participant or guardian, and when clinical signs were consistent with trauma (e.g., restorations clearly attributable to trauma, trauma-related discoloration, or scarring), with confirmation was obtained from the participant or guardian. Restorations attributable to caries or developmental discolorations were not classified as trauma-related.

Examiner calibration was performed prior to data collection using standardized clinical scenarios and reference photographs based on Andreasen’s classification [18]. To assess intra-examiner reliability, approximately 10% of participants were randomly selected for re-examination during the study. The re-examination involved repeating the clinical assessment and comparing diagnostic consistency. Cohen’s kappa coefficient was calculated and yielded a value of κ = 0.87, indicating excellent agreement.

A structured questionnaire was administered to children aged 10 years and above, while parents or guardians responded for younger children. The questionnaire was developed specifically for this study, based on a review of previously published instruments related to sports-related orofacial trauma, with selected items adapted and compiled from multiple sources. It was divided into two sections: The first gathered demographic information (gender, age); the second focused on the history of orofacial dental trauma, including injury type, location, associated sport, and sport-related variables. These sport-related data included the use of protective equipment, with participants asked to report whether they used such equipment regularly and consistently during training and competitions, and to specify the type of equipment used (e.g., mouthguards, headgear/face shields). Additional variables included years of experience, the number of training sessions per week, and the duration of each session. All questionnaires were reviewed at the time of data collection to ensure completeness. The English version of the questionnaire is provided as a supplementary file. (Supplementary File 2)

To minimize recall bias, structured and time-specific questions were used, and self-reported injuries were cross-verified with sports club records or parents, where possible. Cross-verification was possible only in a few cases, mainly when parents were physically present at the club or when coaches could reliably confirm the injury history, and was therefore opportunistic rather than systematic, with no formal percentage of verified cases calculated.

Before full implementation, the questionnaire was piloted on a sample group to assess clarity and feasibility. Children under 6 years were excluded due to non-compliance with club requirements and insufficient participation time in sports. Statistical analysis.

sec.title: Statistical analysis
sec.p: All statistical analyses were conducted using IBM SPSS Statistics version 23. Categorical variables were summarized using descriptive statistics, including frequencies and percentages. Chi-square tests were applied to assess associations between categorical variables and the occurrence of dentofacial trauma.

To identify independent predictors of ODT, a multivariate logistic regression analysis was performed. We constructed logistic regression models using both the Enter method and the Forward Wald stepwise method to ensure transparency in variable selection. In the Enter model, all prespecified covariates were included simultaneously. The Forward Wald method was then applied to illustrate the stepwise exclusion of non‑significant predictors. All candidate variables were initially included based on their theoretical relevance and significance in the bivariate analysis (P < 0.05). Odds ratios (ORs) with corresponding 95% confidence intervals (CIs) were reported. Prior to performing the logistic regression analysis, multicollinearity among independent variables was assessed using variance inflation factors (VIFs), which demonstrated acceptable values for all variables (VIF < 3), indicating no significant multicollinearity. The logistic regression model fit was evaluated using the Hosmer–Lemeshow goodness-of-fit test, with non-significant results (P > 0.05) indicating an adequate fit. A P-value of ≤ 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
A total of 445 young athletes were included in the study, with detailed demographic and clinical characteristics presented in Table 1. Males predominated, and football was the most commonly practiced sport. Most participants reported intermediate levels of training experience, session frequency and duration. The overall prevalence of orofacial dental trauma (ODT) was 47.4% (95% CI: 42.7%–52.0%), with higher rates observed among athletes engaged in contact sports and those with prolonged training exposure. Notably, athletes with more than five years of experience exhibited a trauma rate of 91.0% (95% CI: 84.2%–97.9%). Despite the proven protective benefits, only 33.9% (95% CI: 29.5%–38.3%) of participants reported regularly and consistently using any form of protective equipment during training and competitions.Table 1
Distribution of demographic and clinical characteristics and Chi-Square analysis of the correlation between orofacial dental trauma and studied variables in Syrian athletes (N = 445)
VariablesN (%)Presence of ODTP-value
YesNo
Number%Number%
Total445 (100%)21147.4%23452.6%
GenderMale289 (65.0%)13540.0%15460.0%0.380
Female156 (35.0%)7658.3%8041.7%
Age6–12 years265 (59.0%)10646.7%15953.3%< 0.001 *
12–16 years180 (41.0%)10548.7%7551.3%
Sport TypeFootball84 (19.0%)5059.5%3440.5%< 0.001 *
Basketball71 (16.0%)4664.8%2535.2%
Volleyball64 (14.0%)2742.2%3757.8%
Boxing76 (17.0%)3343.4%4356.6%
Karate80 (18.0%)2632.5%5467.5%
Gymnastic70 (16.0%)2941.4%4158.6%
Years of experienceLess than 1 year119 (27.0%)3226.9%8773.1%< 0.001 *

Training session per weekLess than 3 Sessions112 (25.0%)5044.6%6255.4%< 0.001 *
3 to 4 Sessions281 (63.0%)11440.6%16759.4%
More than 4 Sessions52 (12.0%)4790.4%59.6%
Session duration.1 h112 (25.2%)3531.2%7768.8%< 0.001 *

Using protective equipmentNo294 (66.1%)15452.4%14047.6%0.004*
Mouthguards114 (25.6%)3934.2%7565.8%
Mouthguards + headgear/face shield37 (8.3%)1848.6%1951.4%
ODT Orofacial Dental Trauma
* a significant difference

Orofacial injuries were observed across multiple anatomical locations, with the dentoalveolar region showing the highest prevalence. Among extraoral soft tissues, the lips and nose were most commonly affected, while gingival injuries were the predominant intraoral finding. Traumatic dental injuries (TDIs) were widespread, particularly affecting the upper teeth, with concussion-type injuries constituting the majority of TDIs, followed by crown fractures and tooth mobility. Detailed distributions are shown in Table 2.Table 2
Distribution of injuries across all anatomical sites within the study population in Syrian athletes (N = 211)
Sites of ODTNumber%
Extraoral soft tissueScalp62.8%
Forehead2813.3%
Eyes167.6%
Cheeks3717.5%
Nose6229.4%
Lips8037.9%
Chin4722.3%
Intraoral soft tissueGingiva5626.5%
Tongue136.2%
Buccal mucosa20.9%
TeethTDIs prevalenceUpper teeth10248.3%
Lower teeth125.7%
TDI typeConcussion5425.6%
Tooth-mobility2210.4%
Crown-fracture3014.2%
Avulsion83.8%
Percentages calculated based on the 211 participants who experienced orofacial dental trauma
ODT Orofacial Dental Trauma, TDIs Traumatic Dental Injuries
Statistical analysis identified significant associations between demographic variables, sport-specific factors, and the prevalence of ODT, as shown in Table 1.

Age was positively correlated with ODT, with prevalence increasing across older age groups (P < 0.001), whereas no significant association was found between gender and ODT prevalence (P = 0.380). The type of sport was significantly associated with ODT prevalence (P < 0.001), with basketball and football exhibiting the highest rates. Training-related variables also showed strong associations, including longer years of experience, a higher number of weekly training sessions, and extended session duration (P < 0.001). Additionally, participants who did not use protective equipment had a considerably higher incidence of ODT (P < 0.001).

To further assess independent associations with ODT, a multivariate logistic regression was performed, including age, sport type, years of experience, training sessions per week, session duration, and use of protective equipment. Multicollinearity diagnostics indicated low correlation among predictors (all VIF < 3). The Hosmer–Lemeshow goodness-of-fit test was non-significant (p = 0.126), suggesting adequate model fit.

Age and session duration lost statistical significance after adjustment and were excluded stepwise to improve model parsimony; their effects were attenuated in the presence of years of experience and weekly training frequency. For transparency, a forced-entry model including all candidate covariates was also tested. Results were consistent with the forward Wald model, with age and session duration losing statistical significance after adjustment. The final forward Wald model is presented in Table 3, while the full forced-entry model is provided in Supplementary Table S1.Table 3
Logistic regression model determining risk factors for orofacial dental injuries in Syrian athletes
VariablesCategoriesP-valueORCI95%
LowerUpper
Sport TypeFootballReference
Basketball0.3571.4250.6713.028
Volleyball0.2970.65900.3011.443
Boxing0.6631.2160.5052.926
Karate0.5790.7940.3521.792
Gymnastic0.014 *0.3950.1880.830
Experience yearsLess than 1 yearReference
1 to 5 years< 0.001 *2.6421.5724.440
More than 5 years< 0.001 *28.15910.27077.211
Training session per weekLess than 3 SessionsReference
3 to 4 Sessions0.2541.4100.7812.544
More than 4 Sessions< 0.001 *9.1213.08826.942
Using protective
EquipmentNOReference
Mouthguard< 0.001 *0.2860.1410.577
Mouthguards + headgear/face shield0.034 *0.3270.1160.918
Reference categories: football, Less than 1 year of experience, Less than 3 Sessions of training per week, and no protective equipment
OR Odds Ratio, CI Confidence Interval
* a significant difference

Compared with football players, gymnasts had lower odds of ODT (OR = 0.395; 95% CI: 0.188–0.830; P = 0.014), whereas athletes in other sports showed no significant difference. Athletes with 1–5 years of experience had higher odds than those with less than one year (OR = 2.642; 95% CI: 1.572–4.440; P < 0.001), and those with over five years of participation had much higher odds (OR = 28.159; 95% CI: 10.270–77.211; P < 0.001). Training more than four times per week was associated with greater odds of experiencing ODT compared to those training fewer than three times weekly (OR = 9.121; 95% CI: 3.088–26.942; P < 0.001),

Using protective equipment demonstrated a protective association. Wearing a mouthguard (OR = 0.286; 95% CI: 0.141–0.577; P < 0.001), or combined use of mouthguards with headgear or face shields (OR = 0.327; 95% CI: 0.116–0.918; P = 0.034) was associated with significantly lower odds of ODT.

These associations are visually summarized in Fig. 1, which displays a Forest plot of the odds ratios and 95% confidence intervals for each variable included in the final regression model. Factors with ORs greater than 1 indicate increased odds of injury (risk factors), whereas ORs less than 1 represent protective associations.Fig. 1
Forest Plot of Odds Ratios and 95% Confidence Intervals for Variables Associated with Orofacial Dental Trauma. Note: Variables with confidence intervals entirely on one side of the reference line (OR=1) indicate statistically significant associations

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: This research aimed to assess the prevalence of orofacial dental trauma among children and adolescent athletes in Damascus, Syria, and to explore associated demographic, sports-related, and behavioral risk factors. While the risk of facial and dental injuries in contact sports is well documented [6, 13], data on Syrian youth remain limited. Our findings reveal a notably high ODT prevalence of 47.4%, significantly correlated with sport type, training intensity, and inconsistent use of protective equipment.

Comparative findings across studies are challenging due to methodological differences, including variations in injury definitions, sample characteristics, data collection methods, level of competition, populations studied, injury reporting periods, sports investigated, and reporting standards [19].

Despite these differences, the ODT prevalence observed in our study exceeds most regional and international reports, which typically range between 15% and 30% [11, 13, 20–22]. However, exceptionally high rates have been documented in some contexts, such as a Portuguese study reporting 73% among basketball players [12], which may reflect differences in sport type, age range, and adherence to protective practices.

Beyond methodological differences, the relatively high ODT prevalence observed in this study likely reflects contextual factors unique to Syria. Limited access to affordable protective equipment, inadequate safety regulations, and low awareness among athletes and coaches contribute to elevated injury risk. The prolonged impact of the Syrian conflict and the ongoing economic crisis have further weakened healthcare and sports infrastructures, disrupted school-based preventive programs, and constrained families’ ability to invest in protective gear such as custom-fitted mouthguards [23–25]. These socioeconomic and post-conflict challenges not only explain the disparity between Syrian and international findings but also underline the study’s broader contribution to global sports dentistry. By demonstrating how socioeconomic instability, inadequate safety infrastructure, and post-conflict recovery conditions shape injury patterns and prevention effectiveness, this research extends current understanding beyond high-income contexts and highlights the need for adaptable, context-sensitive preventive strategies.

The injury patterns identified align with existing sports trauma literature. The lips (37.9%) and nose (29.4%) presented as the most common extraoral soft tissue traumas, likely due to their anatomical prominence and lack of protection [12, 26–28]. Intraorally, gingival injuries were more common than tongue injuries, contrasting with findings of Frontera et al. [28], possibly due to differences in methodology and sport types.

Upper teeth experienced more trauma than lower teeth, as reported in several studies [12, 14, 28–30], reflecting their increased anatomical exposure. Concussions were the most commonly reported type of TDI, followed by crown fractures, and avulsions were the least frequent. This contrasts with other studies where crown fractures predominated [14, 21, 28, 30–33]. The discrepancy may result from reliance on self-reports and basic clinical examinations without radiographic confirmation.

No statistically significant association was found between gender and the ODT prevalence, which contrasts with several studies reporting higher injury rates among males [11, 13, 14, 26, 31]. This variation may be attributed to individual biological differences, such as strength, body mass, and psychological traits, as well as sport-specific coaching strategies and gameplay dynamics [34]. However, the present results align with findings by Moreira et al. [12], who reported no significant gender differences in ODT risk.

Older adolescents exhibited a higher prevalence of ODT, consistent with previous research [14, 26, 35], linking this trend to longer sports participation and more intensive training. Additionally, hormonal changes during adolescence—particularly increased androgen levels—contribute to greater muscle mass gains, speed, and strength, but coordination is temporarily compromised during physical development during the adolescent years [36, 37]. This physiological imbalance may increase susceptibility to musculoskeletal and orofacial injuries in older youth athletes.

Sport type and contact level had a clear influence on ODT prevalence [38]. Basketball showed the highest injury rate (64.8%), followed by football (59.5%), consistent with findings from Singh et al. [39]. These findings confirm that dynamic, contact-heavy sports carry increased orofacial injury risks [40]. Injury rates in these sports exceed some previous reports [11, 26, 32], likely due to differences in study populations or methodologies.

In contrast, karate and gymnastics had considerably lower prevalence rates, possibly due to stricter contact regulations and greater emphasis on body control and protective training. The rates of orofacial injuries 43.4% in boxing and 32.5% in karate, were higher than some studies [20, 21, 27], but lower than others [11, 33], suggesting variability across populations and research designs.

Interestingly, gymnastics demonstrated an injury prevalence of 41.4% in this study, which is substantially higher than previously reported rates ranging from 3.6% to 8.3% [11, 41]. This discrepancy may reflect differences in injury mechanisms between collision-based and technical sports or the underreporting of minor injuries in gymnastics. However, logistic regression analysis indicated that gymnastics participation was associated with significantly lower odds of ODT, suggesting that the structured and well-supervised nature of gymnastics training may provide a protective effect despite the sport’s inherent challenges. Nevertheless, the apparent paradox between the relatively high crude prevalence of ODT in gymnastics and the lower adjusted odds ratio observed in regression analysis may reflect residual confounding. Younger age profiles of gymnasts, differences in training intensity, and the possibility that some gymnastics injuries involve anatomical regions other than the orofacial area could partly explain this discrepancy.

Consistent with previous research [11, 34, 42], this study demonstrated a clear dose-response association between longer years of experience and ODT, likely reflecting the cumulative effects of longer exposure, greater competition intensity, and a gradual decline in adherence to safety protocols. Similarly, training frequency was significantly associated with ODT only among athletes with higher weekly training loads, aligning with findings from earlier studies [35, 43, 44]. This association may be explained by fatigue accumulation, reduced recovery time, and impaired neuromuscular coordination, which could compromise technical performance and reflexive protective mechanisms [32].

Lack of protective gear was strongly associated with a higher prevalence of ODT. Mouthguards and facial gear showed a clear protective effect, consistent with international evidence emphasizing their role in preventing orofacial trauma across both contact and non-contact sports [45–48]. Nevertheless, only one-third of participants (33.9%) reported using any form of protection. Low compliance likely stems from discomfort, lack of awareness, and insufficient enforcement by coaches and institutions [2, 3, 10]. This highlights a critical preventive gap that should be addressed through targeted education, effective policy enforcement, and increased accessibility to protective equipment.

The importance of this study lies in being the first of its kind in Syria, providing valuable baseline data on orofacial dental trauma among young athletes and serving as a foundation for future research and comparative analyses. It also highlights the dangers associated with participating in such sports and emphasizes the urgent need for stronger safety regulations and improved protective equipment.

Policy recommendations include enforcing mandatory mouthguard use in high-risk sports by national sports bodies and educational institutions, integrating routine dental screenings into school and club programs, and ensuring careful regulation of training loads, particularly for athletes with prolonged participation. Coordinated educational initiatives for coaches, parents, and athletes are essential to promote early recognition and proper management of dental injuries. These align with international guidelines such as those of the American Academy of Pediatric Dentistry (AAPD) [7].

However, translating international recommendations and the feasibility of implementing such preventive measures into practice in Syria may be challenging. Economic constraints restrict access to custom-fitted mouthguards, while limited awareness among athletes, parents, and coaches, and institutional gaps such as the absence of national sports safety policies and insufficient oral health infrastructure, further hinder feasibility. Therefore, these recommendations should be viewed as long-term policy goals, with immediate priorities focusing on context-appropriate strategies such as subsidized or school-provided mouthguards, awareness campaigns targeting coaches and families, and gradual inclusion of dental screening within school and club health programs. Such incremental steps could help overcome current barriers and lay the groundwork for future national enforcement once infrastructure and policy frameworks are established.

While this study offers valuable findings, several limitations should be acknowledged. First, its cross-sectional design prevents the establishment of causal relationships between the identified risk factors and the occurrence of ODT. Second, reliance on self-reported data introduces potential recall and reporting biases, particularly regarding injury history and protective equipment use. Although structured and time-specific questions were used and self-reported injuries were cross-verified with club records or parents when possible, systematic verification was not feasible, and no formal percentage of verified cases was calculated. Thus, recall bias remains a concern, especially given the lifetime recall period among experienced athletes.

Furthermore, clustering by club was not accounted for in the statistical analysis, which may potentially affect the independence of observations and the precision of estimates. The regional and urban nature of the sample—being restricted to sports clubs in Damascus—also limits the generalizability of the findings to rural or displaced populations who may experience greater socioeconomic hardship, reduced access to protective equipment, and limited availability of dental care. Consequently, the true burden of ODT in Syria may be underestimated.

Methodological inconsistencies across studies, including variations in injury definitions, population characteristics, data collection methods, and sport-specific environments, pose challenges for direct comparison with existing literature.

Although radiographs were not taken, clinical signs were recorded as trauma-related only when they matched the participant’s/guardian’s history. This combined clinical–historical verification reduced the likelihood of misclassification. However, the study did not collect data on socioeconomic disparities, equipment affordability, or temporal differences related to the Syrian conflict onset, limiting the ability to directly assess the impact of these context-specific factors. Importantly, as this is the first study of orofacial dental trauma among athletes in Syria, no pre-conflict data were available for comparison. This absence of baseline information restricts the ability to evaluate temporal changes or the direct impact of the conflict on injury patterns.

Additionally, several potential confounders were not assessed, including socioeconomic status, access to dental care, prior history of dental trauma, and anatomical risk factors such as overjet and lip competence. The omission of these variables may have influenced the observed associations, and results should be interpreted with caution. Moreover, the study did not assess injury severity, emergency treatment administration, long-term sequelae, or economic costs, which limits the ability to evaluate the full clinical and financial burden of ODT. Finally, given the number of comparisons performed across sport types, training variables, and protective equipment, the possibility of Type I error cannot be fully excluded, and the exploratory nature of the analyses should be considered when interpreting the results.

Taken together, these limitations highlight the need for more comprehensive approaches in future research. Longitudinal and cohort designs that track injury incidence and recurrence are recommended to clarify causal relationships and long-term outcomes. Considering clustering effects will further enhance the reliability and generalizability of the findings. To minimize recall bias, the use of prospective injury surveillance systems, coach-reported data, and verification information through medical or dental records is recommended. Establishing national surveillance systems would facilitate comparisons across regions and socioeconomic groups, supporting equitable policy planning and resource allocation.

Future research should also incorporate detailed measures of injury severity, treatment pathways, adherence to protective equipment, and cost analyses to provide a more comprehensive understanding of ODT and its implications. Including socioeconomic indicators, evaluating equipment affordability, and comparing athletes who began training before and after 2011 may better reflect the impact of conflict and economic instability. Importantly, as this is the first study of ODT among athletes in Syria, no pre-conflict baseline data were available, which limits temporal comparisons.

Moreover, future studies should extend beyond urban club-based samples to include rural and displaced populations, as well as other low-resource settings, to provide a more representative picture of ODT and to inform equitable prevention and policy strategies. Qualitative and economic studies that explore the perceptions of athletes, parents, and coaches, as well as the financial burden of ODT on families and healthcare systems, would offer valuable insights for developing context-specific, evidence-based prevention strategies in Syria.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: This study highlights a high prevalence of orofacial dental trauma (47.4%) among child and adolescent athletes in Damascus, and demonstrates significant associations with factors such as sport type, years of experience, training sessions frequency, and the use of protective equipment. Age showed significance in the bivariate analysis but not in regression, while no significant association was observed with gender.

Sports such as basketball and football present a particularly high prevalence due to the intensity of training and inadequate usage of protective equipment. The results provide strong support for initiatives like athlete education, policy reforms, and mandatory mouthguard use.

Given the long-term consequences of dental trauma, addressing it as a public health priority remains essential. Furthermore, longitudinal studies across multiple regions are needed to confirm causal relationships and to guide the development of tailored, evidence-based prevention strategies.

fn.p: Springer Nature remains neutral with regard to jurisdictional claims in published maps and institutional affiliations.

notes.title: Authors’ contributions
notes.p: Taimaa Riad Hamdan: collected the data, did the clinical work, interpreted the data, wrote the manuscript, and participated in designing the study.Lana Anwar Alshayeb: wrote the manuscript, performed the statistical analysis, and participated in designing the study. Mayssoon Dashash: participated as the research supervisor and critically reviewed the manuscript. All authors have read and approved the final manuscript.

notes.title: Data availability
notes.p: The data provided for the results presented in this study is available through the corresponding author upon request.

notes.title: Ethics approval and consent to participate
notes.p: The ethical approval of this study was obtained by the Scientific Research Committee of the Faculty of Dentistry at the University of Damascus, Syria (IRB No. UDDS-2950-10452023/SRC-1550). Additionally, written permission was also obtained from the General Sports Federation of Syria.
Written informed consent was obtained from the parents or legal guardians of all participants prior to enrollment.

All procedures were carried out following the Helsinki Declaration. The study was performed following the ethical standards and followed the Strengthening the Reporting of Observational Studies in Epidemiology (STROBE) guidelines.

notes.title: Competing interests
notes.p: The authors declare no competing interests.
